title: Quick check: What a Gigafactory is

questions.prompt: What are the four defining properties of a Tesla Gigafactory (vs. just 'a big factory')?

choices.text: Square footage, location, employee count, marketing.

GWh-scale annual capacity (energy throughput as the unit), integrated vertical stack (cells + packs + vehicles + energy products on one campus), co-located suppliers (physically on or near the site), single nervous system (shared software stack + culture across sites).

questions.explanation: The 'giga' name references GWh-scale battery output, but the operational differentiator is the integration: vertical stack, supplier co-location, and shared software across sites. Without those, a large factory is just a large factory.

questions.prompt: Why does Tesla co-locate cell production (or cell suppliers) with vehicle assembly at the same Gigafactory?

choices.text: Cells are heavy and bulky relative to their value; shipping them far is expensive and slow. Co-location means cells reach pack assembly via short trips on-site, reducing inventory, logistics cost, and FX risk. Also: when engineers see a problem, they can talk to the cell supplier engineer five minutes away, not in another time zone. Iteration cycles compress.

questions.explanation: Co-location compresses logistics and iteration cycles. Cells are heavy + bulky → shipping cost dominates. Suppliers next door → engineering coordination is in-person, not over Zoom across time zones. Combined: significantly lower per-vehicle cost and faster improvement.
